retrieved_docs.title: Brachycephaly -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Apert syndrome: Bicoronal synostosis with syndactyly of digits 2, 3, and 4. [29] Patients may have mental retardation and mid-face hypoplasia.

retrieved_docs.title: Trigonocephaly -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical correction provides adequate cosmetic results in the majority of patients. Comparison at two years of age of the endoscopic strip craniectomy followed by helmet use and the open skull reconstruction showed that both offer an excellent head shape outcome. [72] The majority of surgically corrected patients have academic achievement near the national mean and above-average intelligence quotient; however, neurocognitive function correlates with the severity of craniosynostosis. [73] The mortality rate for all types of craniosynostosis is well below 1%. [3] [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: When dealing with an infant with macrocephaly, it is important to differentiate isolated macrocephaly from other possible head shapes. Plagiocephaly refers to a flattening of one area of the skull and is often seen in children with a history of lying with the head in one position. Craniosynostosis results from the early closure of cranial sutures, resulting in several other types of head shapes. Brachycephaly results from the premature closure of the coronal suture and results in a broad head with a recessed forehead. Trigonocephaly is the result of premature closure of the metopic suture and gives the forehead a triangular appearance. Scaphocephaly comes from the early closure of the sagittal suture, which has a clinical presentation of anterior-posterior elongation with bitemporal narrowing. Other congenital deformities of the skull can be results of trauma or forces applied to the head while in utero due to multiple gestation, abnormal fetal position, or oligohydramnios.

retrieved_docs.title: Brachycephaly -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Saethre-Chotzen syndrome: Also called "acrocephalosyndactyly," craniosynostosis and or syndactyly of certain digits and characterized by a low-set hairline and facial asymmetry. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Trigonocephaly -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Surgery for trigonocephaly is advocated before the age of 1 year for better cosmetic results. [81] There is still little consensus in cases of mild trigonocephaly. In a survey sent to 102 craniofacial surgeons, there is a disparity of opinion regarding the management of mild nonsyndromic metopic craniosynostosis. [82] However, all of them agreed to operate in moderate and severe cases. Most surgeons prefer open procedures, but the approach used varies. The ideal timing for orbital advancement is still debated; however, most prefer to perform it between 6-10 months of age. [82]

retrieved_docs.title: Brachycephaly -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Positional brachycephaly primarily raises aesthetic concerns. Reliable evidence of its effect on the neurological development of affected infants is lacking. Conversely, untreated synostotic brachycephaly may impede volumetric brain growth and development, is associated with elevated intracranial pressure in infants, and has a high incidence of intracranial hypertension in older untreated patients. [34] Cognitive and behavioral difficulties in those affected have been previously suggested to be related to elevated ICP. [10] However, no correlation was identified between both variables in several other studies. [35] [36] [37] The effect of craniosynostosis surgical repair on neurodevelopment remains widely debated.

retrieved_docs.title: Craniosynostosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of metopic craniosynostosis, the differential diagnosis includes benign metopic ridging, which has been described as an intermediate phenotype between metopic craniosynostosis and normal anatomy [45] [46] and does not require surgical intervention. True metopic craniosynostosis typically has a narrow forehead, biparietal widening, and hypotelorism. A retrospective study found a narrow forehead and pterional constriction in all patients with metopic craniosynostosis but in only 11.2% and 2.8% of patients with metopic ridging, respectively. The study also found that patients with metopic craniosynostosis were more likely to present before 6 months compared to those with benign metopic ridging. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Brachycephaly -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Crouzon syndrome: Bicoronal synostosis resulting in brachycephaly, proptosis, midface hypoplasia with a characteristic underbite (mal-occlusion), and an upturned nose. [28] Patients will usually have normal intelligence.

retrieved_docs.title: Trigonocephaly -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Untreated patients show problems with cognitive functioning when compared to healthy controls. [74] Patients with untreated severe metopic craniosynostosis have been found to have reduced auditory processing of language stimuli in the frontal cortex, suggesting that the orbitofrontal deformity has a negative effect on the frontal cortex. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Brachycephaly -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Carpenter syndrome: Craniosynostosis, syndactyly of certain digits or polydactyly. [32] May have short stature and congenital heart defects.

retrieved_docs.title: Syndactyly -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Syndactyly is typically easy to diagnose on physical exam; however, congenital hand deformities and syndromic conditions should be considered when evaluating a child for syndactyly. Differential diagnosis may include the following: Poland syndrome Apert syndrome Ectrodactyly (cleft hand) Constriction band syndrome Synpolydactyly Carpenter syndrome Oculodentodigital dysplasia

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Pertinent clinical points relevant to HPE are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Brachycephaly -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Muenke syndrome: Coronal craniosynostosis, carpal synostosis, hearing loss, developmental delay, cone-shaped epiphysis, and high narrow palate.

retrieved_docs.title: Brachycephaly -- History and Physical -- Anterior Plagiocephaly
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior plagiocephaly is secondary to unilateral coronal suture craniosynostosis with contralateral forehead bossing.

retrieved_docs.title: Lissencephaly -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Lissencephaly has different levels of severity and symptoms. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Neurosonography Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment -- Congenital Malformations
retrieved_docs.content: Holoprosencephaly: Holoprosencephaly (HPE) results from failure of cleavage of the prosencephalon into telencephalon and diencephalon at 5 weeks of gestation. [9] HPE is a spectrum of malformations with very variable clinical manifestations. It is usually associated with midline facial anomalies such as cleft lip and palate and cyclopia ("the face predicts the brain"). [9] It is classically divided into three subtypes, alobar, semilobar, and lobar types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation is classified as a protracted (prolapsed) arm of the fetus, with the episode of care unspecified or not applicable.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: these specific presentations, creating a hierarchical structure that integrates Face or brow presentation, Prolapsed arm of fetus, Transverse or oblique presentation, and Unspecified malposition or malpresentation under a unified framework. This integrated representation supports clinicians in identifying, categorizing, and managing various abnormal fetal presentations to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2317
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة, and الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة is clinically associated with بضع الرحم. This indicates that an unidentified type of caesarean section scar may increase the risk of early placental separation, which in turn is linked to uterine atony, underscoring the need for vigilant obstetric assessment in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are distinct subtypes of uterine function, each describing a specific asynclitic presentation of the fetal head during labor. Both conditions are considered child concepts within the broader category of uterine function, reflecting variations in fetal head alignment that can influence the mechanics of childbirth and guide obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: “has a broader relationship” connection, underscoring that abnormal presentations such as face or brow, prolapsed arm, and transverse/oblique positions are all encompassed within the overall category of fetal malposition. Collectively, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure in which specific abnormal fetal presentations are organized under generic presentation terms, which in turn are subsumed by the overarching classification of malposition; malpresentation, a key concept for understanding and managing unstable fetal positions during obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: scarring into a unified classification scheme that supports recognition, documentation, and management of pregnancy‑related complications across multiple languages and clinical decision‑making pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2521
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are specific manifestations that fall under the broader concept of uterine function. Uterine function also includes the conditions الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation) and رحم ناقص التوتر (a uterus with reduced tone). Early placental separation is clinically associated with بضع الرحم (uterine atony), indicating that loss of uterine tone can predispose to premature detachment of the placenta. Recognizing how these forms of asynclitism, uterine functional abnormalities, and their clinical associations interrelate helps clinicians anticipate delivery complications and manage uterine tone to improve maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 963
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is an instance of the broader category “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that this fetal presentation can occur regardless of the specific clinical episode. The “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” category is classified as a type of “Malposition; malpresentation,” placing it within the overall group of abnormal fetal positions. Within the same “Malposition; malpresentation” group, other specific abnormalities are also classified, including “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The specific entity “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is linked to the Arabic term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” confirming that it refers to a fetal arm that has prolapsed. Additionally, “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” is an instance of the broader “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” further illustrating the hierarchical organization of these malpresentation categories. Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy in which face or brow presentations, prolapsed fetal arms, and transverse or oblique presentations are all subtypes of malposition or malpresentation, each linked to more general descriptive categories that are independent of specific episodes of care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2418
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are both specific abnormalities that fall under the broader category of uterine function. Within the spectrum of uterine function, secondary uterine atony (عطالة رحمية ثانوية) is also classified as a related condition. Together, these entities represent distinct yet interconnected aspects of uterine functional disorders that can influence the mechanics of labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: lesions, fetal demise, and undefined obstetric indications within a comprehensive framework that guides clinicians in assessing how each factor may affect maternal and fetal outcomes and informs comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 800
retrieved_docs.content: more severe presentations involving fetal malpresentation, pelvic malformations, and abnormal labor. This integrated structure supports comprehensive clinical understanding and facilitates targeted patient care across the full range of obstetric scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2051
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal presentation finding is classified under the broader category of malposition; malpresentation, and malposition; malpresentation is identified as a specific manifestation of عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين (fetal position instability). This hierarchical relationship indicates that abnormalities in fetal presentation are part of the larger spectrum of malposition and malpresentation, which arise from underlying instability of the fetal position.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.
